macOS Time Machine Taking Forever? One Trick to Speed It Up

macOS's Time Machine is a great backup tool, but the backup process can get painfully slow at times — especially noticeable if you haven't run a Time Machine backup in a while. This post is a quick note on the fastest way to get Time Machine moving, so backups stop being a chore.

Back when my machine was fresh, I ran Time Machine every week to avoid the risk of data loss. But work got busy and I went a stretch without backing up. When I finally fired Time Machine back up, the backup process was crawling, sometimes getting stuck on one phase indefinitely.
The screenshot above shows Time Machine looking for changed files.

For a developer, the file churn on your machine is honestly pretty high — you're constantly modifying, compiling, and testing code, which generates huge amounts of file changes. That makes Time Machine spend way more time hunting for changed files before it can back anything up.
The fix is simple, but there's some setup first. I use iTerm, so I need to grant it Full Disk Access. Go to System Settings → Privacy & Security → Full Disk Access.
You'll get a password prompt — enter it and you're done.
